justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Three distinct integration patterns clearly identified — SAP as batch adapter (SFTP/XML/15-min cadence), DHL as bidirectional REST+webhook with async gateway, Analytics as Kafka consumer/producer. (2) DHL 500ms constraint explicitly addressed via webhook gateway that acks with 202 immediately and processes async. (3) DHL webhook idempotency addressed with idempotency store keyed on delivery ID + status, called out in both Risks table and Pitfalls. (4) SAP silent failure risk identified in Risks table with retry, alerting on 3 consecutive failures, and local batch storage. (5) Dedicated integration layer via per-system ACLs, explicitly rejecting a single God integration service. (6) Flexibility points clearly state new integrations require only a new adapter — FedEx example given.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — goes well beyond minimum pass criteria with outbox pattern, DLQ, schema registry, and context map
Correctly identifies all three integration patterns with distinct architectural treatments rather than a one-size-fits-all approach
DHL webhook handling is well-designed: 202 Accepted immediately, async internal processing, explicit idempotency store
SAP silent failure risk addressed with monitoring, alerting on consecutive failures, and local batch storage as fallback
Strong separation of concerns — domain events are protocol-agnostic, transformation lives in ACLs, not in domain services
Pitfalls section directly calls out all failure modes from the rubric as things to avoid
Data flow diagram and interface table make the architecture immediately understandable
Minor: SAP batch file-level checksums or sequence IDs for idempotency not explicitly mentioned (only SFTP retry and alerting)
Confidence assessment is honest about low-confidence areas and assumptions, showing architectural maturity

agent_output_excerpt:
## Executive Summary

The integration architecture uses an **Event-Driven Backbone** with dedicated **Anti-Corruption Layers (ACLs)** per external system. All internal domain events flow through a central event bus (Kafka), and each external system integration is isolated behind its own adapter that translates between the internal ubiquitous language and the external system's protocol, format, and timing constraints. This design ensures no external system's quirks pollute the core domain, and ea
